title: Breast Pathology: Core Principles
summary: Primary Diagnostic Focus: Evaluate lumpectomy margins and sentinel lymph nodes; distinguish invasive carcinoma from benign mimickers.
body:
## Key Points
- Core Teaching Question: Is the lesion malignant, what is the histologic type, and are the margins clear?
- Primary Diagnostic Focus: Evaluate lumpectomy margins and sentinel lymph nodes; distinguish invasive carcinoma from benign mimickers.
- Cardinal Rule: Complex sclerosing lesions and radial scars may mimic invasive carcinoma; correlation with permanent sections and immunostains may be necessary.
- Representative Specimens: Lumpectomy margins, core needle biopsies, sentinel lymph nodes.

## Triad Summary
- Tubular
- Sclerosing
- Cribriform

title: Thyroid Pathology: Core Principles
summary: Primary Diagnostic Focus: Triage thyroid lesions into papillary, follicular‑patterned and other categories; recognize that follicular carcinoma cannot be diagnosed on frozen section.
body:
## Key Points
- Core Teaching Question: Is this thyroid nodule malignant and, if so, should a total thyroidectomy be performed?
- Primary Diagnostic Focus: Triage thyroid lesions into papillary, follicular‑patterned and other categories; recognize that follicular carcinoma cannot be diagnosed on frozen section.
- Cardinal Rule: Follicular carcinoma requires capsular and/or vascular invasion on permanent sections; never overcall invasion on frozen.
- Representative Specimens: Lobectomy, isthmusectomy, subtotal or completion thyroidectomy.

## Triad Summary
- Papillary
- Follicular
- SolidSpindle

title: Gynecologic Pathology: Core Principles
summary: Primary Diagnostic Focus: Classify ovarian and endometrial tumors into major histologic categories; determine need for comprehensive staging or debulking.
body:
## Key Points
- Core Teaching Question: Is the adnexal or uterine mass benign, borderline, or malignant, and does it require staging?
- Primary Diagnostic Focus: Classify ovarian and endometrial tumors into major histologic categories; determine need for comprehensive staging or debulking.
- Cardinal Rule: Borderline serous tumors can be difficult to distinguish from invasive carcinoma on frozen; deferral may be prudent.
- Representative Specimens: Oophorectomy, salpingo‑oophorectomy, hysterectomy, omentectomy.

## Triad Summary
- Serous
- Mucinous
- Endometrioid

title: Head & Neck Pathology: Core Principles
summary: Primary Diagnostic Focus: Distinguish squamous cell carcinoma from benign/inflammatory lesions, recognize papillary thyroid carcinoma metastases and lymphomas in lymph nodes.
body:
## Key Points
- Core Teaching Question: Is this head and neck lesion malignant and what is its histologic type? Are margins clear?
- Primary Diagnostic Focus: Distinguish squamous cell carcinoma from benign/inflammatory lesions, recognize papillary thyroid carcinoma metastases and lymphomas in lymph nodes.
- Cardinal Rule: Reactive squamous atypia and cystic changes can mimic carcinoma; correlation with clinical and radiologic findings is essential.
- Representative Specimens: Mucosal margins, neck dissection lymph nodes, thyroid lobectomy frozen sections.

## Triad Summary
- SquamousHN
- PapillaryThyroid
- Lymphoma

title: Liver/Biliary Pathology: Core Principles
summary: Primary Diagnostic Focus: Differentiate primary hepatocellular patterns from metastatic tumors and benign regenerative nodules; assess bile duct lesions for malignancy.
body:
## Key Points
- Core Teaching Question: Is this hepatic or bile duct lesion malignant and are surgical margins free of tumor?
- Primary Diagnostic Focus: Differentiate primary hepatocellular patterns from metastatic tumors and benign regenerative nodules; assess bile duct lesions for malignancy.
- Cardinal Rule: Poorly differentiated hepatocellular carcinoma may mimic cholangiocarcinoma; correlation with radiology and immunostains on permanents is essential.
- Representative Specimens: Segmental hepatectomy margins, wedge biopsies of hepatic nodules, bile duct excisions.

## Triad Summary
- Hepatocellular
- Cholangiocarcinoma
- MetastaticLiver

title: Neurosurgical Pathology: Core Principles
summary: Primary Diagnostic Focus: Provide rapid classification of CNS lesions into glioma, metastatic tumor, or meningioma to guide extent of surgery.
body:
## Key Points
- Core Teaching Question: What is the nature of this central nervous system lesion and does it require further resection?
- Primary Diagnostic Focus: Provide rapid classification of CNS lesions into glioma, metastatic tumor, or meningioma to guide extent of surgery.
- Cardinal Rule: Frozen sections distort brain tissue; cytologic crush preparations may be more reliable for grading glial tumors.
- Representative Specimens: Stereotactic biopsies, intraoperative tumor debulking specimens.

## Triad Summary
- Glioma
- MetastasisCNS
- Meningioma

title: Pancreas Pathology: Core Principles
summary: Primary Diagnostic Focus: Distinguish inflammation and benign cystic lesions from invasive carcinoma; evaluate margins for tumor.
body:
## Key Points
- Core Teaching Question: Is the pancreatic resection margin free of invasive carcinoma and is this lesion malignant or benign?
- Primary Diagnostic Focus: Distinguish inflammation and benign cystic lesions from invasive carcinoma; evaluate margins for tumor.
- Cardinal Rule: Desmoplastic stroma can mimic chronic pancreatitis on frozen; caution with diagnosing adenocarcinoma without definitive glands invading stroma.
- Representative Specimens: Pancreatic neck margin from Whipple procedure, distal pancreatectomy margins, enucleation of cystic lesions.

## Triad Summary
- Glandular
- Ductal
- Desmoplastic

title: Lung Pathology: Core Principles
summary: Primary Diagnostic Focus: Provide rapid triage for malignancy versus benign/reactive lesions; do not overinterpret frozen artifacts.
body:
## Key Points
- Core Teaching Question: Is this lung lesion malignant and, if so, should more lung tissue be resected?
- Primary Diagnostic Focus: Provide rapid triage for malignancy versus benign/reactive lesions; do not overinterpret frozen artifacts.
- Cardinal Rule: Many subtle adenocarcinoma patterns (e.g. lepidic) are under‑diagnosed on frozen; deferral may be appropriate.
- Representative Specimens: Wedge resections for peripheral nodules, mediastinal lymph node biopsies, lobectomies.

## Triad Summary
- Adenocarcinoma
- Squamous
- SmallCell

title: Renal Pathology: Core Principles
summary: Primary Diagnostic Focus: Distinguish clear cell renal cell carcinoma from oncocytoma and other benign lesions; evaluate partial nephrectomy margins.
body:
## Key Points
- Core Teaching Question: Is the renal mass malignant, and if so, what subtype is it; are margins free of tumor?
- Primary Diagnostic Focus: Distinguish clear cell renal cell carcinoma from oncocytoma and other benign lesions; evaluate partial nephrectomy margins.
- Cardinal Rule: Papillary and chromophobe renal tumors may appear deceptively bland; rely on architectural patterns more than cytology alone.
- Representative Specimens: Partial nephrectomy margins, radical nephrectomy sampling, ureteral margins.

## Triad Summary
- ClearCell
- PapillaryKidney
- Chromophobe
